original_abstract: Autonomous agents have long been a focal point of research in both academia and industry. Traditional approaches often rely on training agents within isolated environments using limited knowledge, a method that diverges significantly from human learning and frequently fails to produce human-like decision-making. Recently, the rise of large language models (LLMs) trained on massive amounts of web-scale data has unlocked new potential for achieving human-level intelligence, sparking a surge of interest in LLM-based autonomous agents. In this paper, we provide a systematic and holistic review of this rapidly evolving field. We begin by proposing a unified framework for the construction of these agents, which synthesizes various methodologies into a coherent architecture. We then offer a comprehensive overview of how these agents are being applied across diverse domains, including the social sciences, natural sciences, and engineering. Furthermore, we examine the current strategies used for evaluating agent performance and intelligence. Finally, by identifying existing bottlenecks and synthesizing recent progress, we outline key challenges and suggest promising future directions for research in the field.
humanized_abstract: The topic of autonomous agents is not new to either the academic or the commercial world. Most current implementations train their agents in some simulator environment, using an assortment of learned and hand-coded knowledge, which are almost always based on non-human, non-intuitive ways of making decisions and hence do not lead to truly human-level decision making behavior. With the more recent advances in large language models (LLMs) trained on web-scale amounts of data, the interest in developing LLM-based autonomous agents has surged and we provide here a systematic and in-depth review of this increasingly growing area of research. We first outline a new unifying approach for constructing such agents while embedding existing works into a new unified architecture. Then, we give an overview of current applications of agent-based research spanning from the social and natural sciences as well as engineering. We follow that by discussing approaches to measure and assess the performance and intelligence of these agents. Finally, based on current challenges and following a reflection of the recent advances in the field, we highlight the key research avenues that need to be addressed in this exciting area.
